h1Title: Rotator Cuff Tendinopathy: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Rotator cuff tendinopathy is a degenerative pathology of one or more of the four rotator cuff tendons — most commonly the supraspinatus — characterized by tendon fiber disruption, neovascularization, and failed healing response within the subacromial space. It is the leading cause of shoulder pain in adults, accounting for 44–65% of all shoulder complaints, with a prevalence of 17–34% in the working-age population rising to above 50% in people over 70. The condition produces a painful arc of abduction, weakness of shoulder elevation and external rotation, and functional limitation in overhead and reaching tasks. Physiotherapy with progressive tendon loading is the evidence-based first-line treatment; corticosteroid injections provide short-term pain relief; surgery is reserved for cases failing 3–6 months of supervised rehabilitation.

keyStats.prevalence: 17–34% of working-age adults; up to 54% prevalence on imaging in asymptomatic adults over 70 (Tempelhof et al. J Shoulder Elbow Surg 1999)
keyStats.demographics: Peak clinical diagnosis age 45–65; overhead workers (painters, electricians, overhead athletes) at 3–5× higher occupational risk; slightly higher in women
keyStats.avgAge: Onset of symptomatic tendinopathy typically age 40–60; rotator cuff pathology increases exponentially with each decade after 40
keyStats.globalCases: Shoulder disorders are the third most common musculoskeletal complaint in primary care globally; rotator cuff pathology accounts for approximately 70% of shoulder diagnoses in specialist clinics

definition: Rotator cuff tendinopathy (ICD-10: M75.1) is a spectrum of pathological changes in the tendons of the rotator cuff — supraspinatus, infraspinatus, teres minor, and subscapularis — characterized by failed tendon healing, collagen disorganisation, neovascularisation, and increased non-collagenous ground substance without classical inflammatory cellular infiltrate. The term has largely replaced 'tendinitis' in the research and clinical literature because histopathologic studies consistently show degenerative (angiofibroblastic) changes rather than acute inflammation. The supraspinatus tendon is involved in over 80% of cases, typically within its 'critical zone' of relative avascularity 1–2 cm proximal to the insertion on the greater tuberosity. The condition exists on a continuum from reactive tendinopathy (reversible with load reduction) to degenerative tendinopathy (partial or full-thickness structural failure). Associated subacromial bursitis — inflammation of the bursa separating the rotator cuff from the acromion and coracoacromial ligament — frequently coexists and contributes to pain. Subacromial impingement syndrome is a clinical term encompassing rotator cuff tendinopathy and bursitis from mechanical compression under the coracoacromial arch; the AHA-ASA and NICE now prefer the non-mechanistic terms tendinopathy and bursitis. Physical medicine and rehabilitation specialists, orthopaedic surgeons, and physiotherapists manage the condition.

typesClassification.description: Short-duration response to acute overload; collagen architecture largely intact but cells upregulate matrix protein production. Reversible with relative rest and graduated load reduction. Typically resolves within 4–8 weeks with conservative management.

Reactive phase not resolved; progressive collagen disorganization, matrix degeneration, and initial neovascularization. Partially reversible with structured progressive loading rehabilitation over 12–16 weeks.

Established collagen disarray, focal cell death, and extensive neovascularisation — the histopathologic hallmark of chronic tendinopathy. Risk of partial or full-thickness tear increases substantially. Response to loading rehabilitation is slower and less complete.

Calcium apatite deposits form within the degenerating supraspinatus tendon; occurs in 2–8% of the general population and 15–40% of patients with shoulder pain. Resorptive phase (calcium turning milky/chalky) produces the most acute pain. Treated with needling, barbotage, or shockwave therapy.

primarySymptoms: Painful arc of active shoulder abduction between 60° and 120° — pain comes on as the greater tuberosity passes beneath the coracoacromial arch and typically eases above 120° when the tuberosity clears the arch.
Shoulder pain at night, particularly when lying on the affected side, often described as a deep aching in the outer arm and deltoid region, frequently interrupting sleep.
Weakness of shoulder abduction and external rotation proportional to the degree of tendon fiber disruption, limiting overhead tasks, reaching behind the back, and lifting objects away from the body.
Pain that worsens with overhead activities — painting, throwing, swimming, serving in racquet sports, or reaching to a high shelf.
Tenderness to palpation at or just proximal to the greater tuberosity insertion of the supraspinatus tendon.

Positive impingement signs: Neer sign (pain on passive forward flexion to 180° with internal rotation) and Hawkins-Kennedy test (pain on passive internal rotation with the shoulder at 90° forward flexion) are the most validated clinical tests.
Gradual onset over weeks to months in most cases, often preceded by a period of increased overhead activity, swimming frequency, or return to sport.
Limited active elevation ROM compared with passive ROM — a discrepancy of more than 30° suggests partial rotator cuff tear rather than pure tendinopathy.
Crepitus or clicking in the subacromial space with shoulder rotation — from roughened tendon surface or degenerative bursitis.
earlyWarningSigns: Intermittent posterior shoulder aching after overhead sport or work sessions that resolves with rest but recurs predictably — characteristic of reactive tendinopathy before it progresses to dysrepair.
First appearance of a painful arc at 80–100° abduction without shoulder weakness — suggests subacromial bursitis without significant tendon fiber disruption.
Night shoulder aching that begins to prevent sleep position changes — often precedes the full clinical presentation by several weeks.
emergencySigns: Sudden complete loss of active shoulder elevation after a specific injury (e.g. falling on an outstretched arm) with preserved passive ROM — suggests acute full-thickness rotator cuff tear requiring urgent orthopaedic referral for timely surgical repair.

Progressive wasting of the deltoid and supraspinatus fossa with severe shoulder weakness — may indicate suprascapular nerve entrapment or brachial neuritis (Parsonage-Turner syndrome) rather than tendinopathy.
Fever, hot erythematous shoulder, and severe constant pain — septic bursitis or septic arthritis requires urgent aspiration and antibiotics.

causes.cause: Mechanical compression under the coracoacromial arch
causes.description: Narrowing of the subacromial space during shoulder elevation, internal rotation, or anterior tipping of the scapula compresses the supraspinatus tendon between the humeral head and the coracoacromial arch. Repetitive impingement causes mechanical trauma and progressive collagen disorganization. Subacromial space narrowing is exacerbated by a hooked (type III) acromion, acromioclavicular joint osteophytes, or scapular dyskinesis.

Tendon vascularity decreases progressively after age 30, particularly in the 'critical zone' of the supraspinatus. Reduced cellular repair capacity, glycosaminoglycan changes, and lipid deposition promote matrix disorganization. Post-mortem studies show degenerative tendon changes in 45–50% of people over 60, many without preceding pain.

causes.cause: Repetitive overhead occupational or sporting loading
causes.description: Painters, electricians, carpenters, and overhead throwing athletes generate high-repetition tensile and compressive loads across the critical zone. NIOSH surveillance consistently identifies overhead workers as the highest-risk occupational group for rotator cuff pathology.

Inadequate scapular upward rotation and posterior tipping reduce subacromial space during arm elevation. Weakness of serratus anterior, lower trapezius, and rotator cuff muscles relative to the upper trapezius and pectoralis minor is the most common correctable biomechanical cause in rehabilitation.

Diabetes mellitus, hypercholesterolaemia, and hypothyroidism independently increase rotator cuff tendinopathy risk through collagen glycation, lipid deposition in tendon matrix, and altered fibroblast metabolism. Smoking reduces tendon vascularity and independently doubles tendinopathy risk.

riskFactors.description: Prevalence increases exponentially with age; imaging studies show rotator cuff pathology in 28% of asymptomatic adults at 60 and 54% at 70+ (Tempelhof et al. 1999).
Overhead workers have 3–5× higher rotator cuff pathology rates; swimmers show supraspinatus tendinopathy in 40–70% of competitive training seasons.
Scapular muscle weakness reduces subacromial space by 2–3 mm during elevation and increases supraspinatus compressive load by 30–40%; addressed by targeted physiotherapy.

Diabetic patients have 2–3× higher rotator cuff tendinopathy rates from advanced glycation end-products altering collagen cross-linking. Poor glycemic control correlates with more severe tendinopathy.
Smokers have approximately double the rate of rotator cuff tendinopathy; nicotine reduces tendon vascularity and impairs fibroblast repair capacity.
Xanthoma-like lipid deposits are found in degenerative rotator cuff tendons; hypercholesterolaemia doubles tendinopathy risk in prospective epidemiological studies.
Type III acromion reduces the subacromial space by 2–4 mm and increases the mechanical impingement risk; present in 30% of adults and associated with a 2× higher rate of full-thickness tear.

affectedDemographics: Adults aged 45–65: the peak decade for symptomatic rotator cuff tendinopathy; working and recreationally active age group experiencing both degenerative and overuse pathways.
Overhead athletes (swimmers, baseball/cricket pitchers, tennis and volleyball players, gymnasts): competitive swimmers report tendinopathy rates of 40–70% during peak training seasons.
Overhead manual workers: painters, electricians, plasterers, and construction workers show 3–5× higher occupational incidence than desk-based workers in NIOSH surveillance data.
People with diabetes: 2–3× higher tendinopathy risk, more severe pathology, and slower response to conservative treatment than non-diabetic populations.
diagnosisOverview: Rotator cuff tendinopathy is diagnosed through clinical assessment and confirmed with imaging. The history identifies characteristic overhead-activity-related pain, a painful arc at 60–120° abduction, and night pain. Clinical examination includes the Neer impingement test (forward flexion to 180° with internal rotation), the Hawkins-Kennedy test (90° forward flexion with passive internal rotation), the empty-can (Jobe) test for supraspinatus strength (resisted abduction at 30° scaption with the thumb pointing down), and the full-can test. The painful arc is assessed by active abduction from 0° to 180°. Sensitivity for impingement-related pathology ranges from 57–72% for individual tests; combining two or more positive tests improves positive predictive value to over 80%. Shoulder plain radiographs are taken to identify acromial morphology (type I flat, II curved, III hooked), acromioclavicular joint osteophytes, calcific deposits in the tendon, and to exclude glenohumeral arthritis. Ultrasound has become the primary imaging tool in most musculoskeletal clinics: it identifies tendon hypoechogenicity, fiber disruption, intratendinous calcification, and dynamic subacromial bursal thickening. Ultrasound sensitivity for partial-thickness tears is 72–87% and for full-thickness tears 97–100% in experienced hands. MRI provides superior evaluation of full-thickness tears, intraosseous cysts, and SLAP labral lesions; it is the preferred pre-operative investigation. Distinguishing tendinopathy from a partial-thickness tear or a full-thickness tear changes management significantly: full-thickness tears should not receive isolated conservative management beyond 3–6 months without surgical consideration.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Provokes subacromial impingement by internally rotating the shoulder at 90° forward flexion; most sensitive clinical test for subacromial pathology (sensitivity 72–79%)
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Clinician places the arm at 90° elevation and applies passive internal rotation. Pain in the anterior or lateral shoulder constitutes a positive result. Takes under 30 seconds.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assess supraspinatus strength and reproduces supraspinatus tendinopathy pain with resisted abduction
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patient holds the arm at 90° in the scapular plane with thumb pointing down (empty-can) or up (full-can); clinician applies downward resistance. Weakness or pain indicates supraspinatus pathology.

diagnosticTests.test: Shoulder plain radiographs (AP with internal/external rotation, supraspinatus outlet, axillary views)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies acromion morphology (type I/II/III), calcific deposits, acromioclavicular osteophytes, and excludes glenohumeral arthritis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Three to four X-ray positions, each taking seconds. The supraspinatus outlet view profiles the coracoacromial arch. No preparation required.

diagnosticTests.test: Shoulder ultrasound with dynamic impingement assessment
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies tendon hypoechogenicity, fiber disruption, calcific deposits, partial or full-thickness tears, and subacromial bursal thickening; guides injection placement
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Handheld transducer applied to the anterior and lateral shoulder in multiple arm positions. Dynamic impingement is assessed by rotating the arm in real time. Takes 15–20 minutes.

diagnosticTests.test: MRI shoulder (with or without intra-articular contrast)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Evaluates full-thickness and partial-thickness tear extent, intraosseous cysts, labral pathology, and biceps tendon disease; primary pre-operative investigation
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 25–40 minutes in the scanner. No contrast is required for most tendinopathy evaluations. Contrast arthrogram improves partial-tear sensitivity when surgery is planned.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes C5–C6 cervical radiculopathy and suprascapular nerve entrapment when progressive shoulder weakness is disproportionate to tendinopathy severity
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Small electrical pulses and fine-needle EMG. Takes 45–60 minutes. Normal NCS/EMG in pure tendinopathy; abnormal in brachial neuritis or nerve entrapment.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of rotator cuff tendinopathy follows a graduated, evidence-based rehabilitation pathway aligned with the British Elbow and Shoulder Society (BESS), NICE, and AAOS guidance. The cornerstone is supervised progressive tendon-loading physiotherapy: a 12–16 week programme of isometric loading, then isotonic eccentric-concentric exercises, and finally sport or occupation-specific loading drives collagen remodelling, improves tendon mechanical properties, and resolves symptoms in 60–80% of patients with moderate to severe tendinopathy. Subacromial corticosteroid injection (triamcinolone 40 mg under ultrasound guidance) provides superior short-term pain relief at 4–6 weeks compared with physiotherapy alone and is most effective when used as a bridge to enable physiotherapy participation. NICE 2019 and AAOS 2018 guidelines both state that corticosteroid injection should not be offered as the primary standalone treatment; it is most effective as an adjunct to supervised exercise. Platelet-rich plasma (PRP) injection has been evaluated in multiple RCTs; evidence quality is moderate and current guidelines do not recommend PRP over corticosteroid for acute tendinopathy. High-volume injection (HVI) — 10 mL corticosteroid + 40 mL normal saline injected into the subacromial space — disrupts neovascularisation and shows efficacy comparable to PRP in degenerative tendinopathy in several UK trials. Extracorporeal shockwave therapy (ESWT) is effective for calcific tendinopathy, with 80–90% resolution of calcific deposits within 3–6 months of focused shockwave treatment; it is also effective for non-calcific tendinopathy with moderate-quality evidence. Surgical subacromial decompression (acromioplasty) is reserved for failure of 3–6 months of supervised rehabilitation; a 2017 Cochrane sham-controlled trial (Beard et al.) showed no significant benefit of acromioplasty over sham surgery for non-calcific tendinopathy, shifting guidelines toward conservative management as the default. Rotator cuff repair surgery is performed for full-thickness tears, especially in patients under 65 with acute traumatic tears.

medicalTreatments.name: Supervised progressive tendon-loading physiotherapy (12–16 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: Phase 1 (weeks 1–4): isometric shoulder abduction exercises to maintain tendon capacity without compressive loading. Phase 2 (weeks 4–12): isotonic eccentric-concentric loading with resistance bands progressing to light weights. Phase 3 (weeks 12–16): sport or occupation-specific loading and plyometric training. Scapular stabilization exercises included throughout.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 70–80% achieving significant pain reduction and functional improvement at 16 weeks in moderate-to-severe tendinopathy; best long-term durability of any treatment modality

medicalTreatments.name: Subacromial corticosteroid injection (triamcinolone 40 mg + 5 mL bupivacaine 0.5%)
medicalTreatments.description: Ultrasound-guided injection into the subacromial-subdeltoid bursa above the supraspinatus tendon. Provides rapid pain control to facilitate physiotherapy. Limited to two injections per 12-month period to avoid tendon toxicity.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Significant superiority over placebo at 4–6 weeks (SMD -0.41); comparable to physiotherapy at 6 months; effect largely dissipated at 12 months (Cochrane 2003)

medicalTreatments.name: NSAIDs (naproxen 500 mg twice daily or diclofenac 50 mg three times daily, 2–4 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: Short-course oral NSAIDs reduce pain from associated subacromial bursitis and enable physiotherapy participation during flares. Topical NSAIDs (diclofenac gel) are an option in patients with GI contraindications.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Moderate pain relief at 2–4 weeks; no sustained effect on tendon structure; no RCT evidence of superiority over physiotherapy as standalone treatment

medicalTreatments.name: Extracorporeal shockwave therapy (ESWT, 3–6 sessions)
medicalTreatments.description: Focused or radial shockwave energy applied to the tendon and calcific deposit; promotes calcium crystal resorption and stimulates tendon matrix remodelling via increased growth factor expression.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 80–90% resolution of calcific deposits at 6 months for calcific tendinopathy (Cacchio et al. 2006); moderate evidence for 25–40% additional pain reduction in non-calcific tendinopathy vs. sham

medicalTreatments.name: Ultrasound-guided barbotage (needling and lavage) for calcific tendinopathy
medicalTreatments.description: A needle is inserted into the calcium deposit under ultrasound guidance; saline is injected and aspirated to fragment and remove the calcium. Often combined with subacromial steroid injection. Highly effective for resorptive-phase calcific tendinopathy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Significant calcium resorption and VAS pain reduction at 1–6 months vs. sham needling; RCT (de Witte et al. 2012) shows 80% excellent outcome at 12 months

medicalTreatments.name: Platelet-rich plasma (PRP) injection
medicalTreatments.description: Autologous PRP prepared from 15–20 mL venous blood and injected into the tendon under ultrasound guidance. Provides growth factors that theoretically stimulate tendon matrix remodelling. Currently offered in specialist clinics for degenerative tendinopathy failing corticosteroid + physio.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Comparable to corticosteroid at 12 months in most RCTs; superior at 24–48 months in 2–3 trials of degenerative tendinopathy; not yet guideline-endorsed over corticosteroid for acute tendinopathy

surgicalOptions.name: Arthroscopic subacromial decompression (acromioplasty)
surgicalOptions.description: Arthroscopic resection of the inferior acromion surface and coracoacromial ligament to enlarge the subacromial space. For refractory non-calcific tendinopathy failing 3–6 months of supervised rehabilitation; note that a 2018 RCT (Beard et al. UK FROST predecessor) showed no benefit over sham surgery, and guidelines have de-emphasised routine acromioplasty.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptom resolution in 60–75% at 1 year for refractory tendinopathy; not superior to sham in controlled trials for non-calcific tendinopathy

surgicalOptions.name: Rotator cuff repair (arthroscopic single- or double-row tendon repair)
surgicalOptions.description: For full-thickness or large partial-thickness rotator cuff tears in appropriate patients. Arthroscopic repair re-attaches the avulsed tendon to the greater tuberosity with suture anchors. Open or mini-open techniques used for massive tears requiring tendon transfer.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Structural repair integrity at 2 years: 75–85% for small-to-medium tears; 50–70% for large tears. Functional improvement (Constant score improvement of 25–40 points) in over 85% at 12 months

alternativeTreatments.name: Low-level laser therapy (LLLT)
alternativeTreatments.description: A 2016 Cochrane review (12 RCTs) found moderate evidence that LLLT (830–980 nm) provides significant short-term pain relief at 4 weeks in rotator cuff tendinopathy compared with sham, with effect sizes comparable to NSAIDs for mild-to-moderate pain.

alternativeTreatments.name: Acupuncture
alternativeTreatments.description: A 2005 systematic review (Green et al. Cochrane) and a 2014 update found limited evidence supporting acupuncture for short-term shoulder pain relief; evidence quality was low but no serious adverse events were reported.

whySeeSpecialist: Referral to a physiatrist, shoulder surgeon, or specialist physiotherapist is indicated when symptoms persist beyond 6 weeks of home exercises, when a partial or full-thickness tear is suspected on imaging, or when a third injection is required. Surgical assessment is warranted after 3–6 months of supervised rehabilitation without adequate improvement.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a physiotherapist with specialist shoulder rehabilitation experience, ideally trained in progressive tendon-loading protocols. For injection therapy, a physiatrist or sports medicine physician performing ultrasound-guided injections provides more accurate subacromial placement. For surgical evaluation, select a shoulder surgeon performing over 100 rotator cuff procedures per year.
hospitalCriteria: Shoulder specialist physiotherapy with progressive loading programme capability
Musculoskeletal ultrasound service for guided injection and diagnostic imaging
Shockwave therapy (ESWT) unit for calcific tendinopathy
MRI with dedicated shoulder coil for pre-operative planning
keyFacilities: Shoulder and sports medicine outpatient rehabilitation units
Sports medicine clinics with shockwave therapy
preventionStrategies: Develop balanced shoulder muscle strength — prioritize lower trapezius, serratus anterior, and external rotator strengthening to maintain adequate subacromial space during overhead activity.
Warm up with rotator cuff activation exercises before overhead sport or work — 10 repetitions of external rotation, Y-T-W scapular exercises, and side-lying external rotation before training.
Modify overhead training volumes in sport — avoid increasing overhead throwing or swimming volume by more than 10% per week; taper volume during rest periods rather than abrupt return to full load.
Optimise metabolic risk factors — control blood glucose in diabetes, treat hypercholesterolaemia, and stop smoking to reduce tendon degeneration rate.

Undergo periodic shoulder assessment in high-risk occupations — annual physiotherapy screening for painters, electricians, and overhead construction workers detects early tendinopathy before it progresses to tears.
lifestyleModifications: Avoid lying directly on the affected shoulder; sleep supine or with a pillow under the arm in 30° of abduction to reduce subacromial pressure.
Perform prescribed tendon-loading exercises daily — the single most important behavioural change for tendon recovery; consistency over 12 weeks produces structural remodelling.
Modify overhead activities to within pain-free range during the acute phase — continue light overhead use but avoid activities that reproduce the painful arc.
Apply ice for 15 minutes after activity-related pain flares; avoid heat during the acute bursitis phase.
Reduce swimming volume by 30–50% and avoid butterfly stroke until supraspinatus tendon rehabilitation has progressed to isotonic phase.
Integrate daily scapular-setting and posture correction into routine — retract and depress the scapulae for 10 seconds every hour during desk-based work to unload the subacromial space.

recommended: Protein intake of 1.2–1.5 g/kg/day to support tendon collagen synthesis during rehabilitation
Vitamin C 500 mg daily — essential cofactor for type I collagen cross-linking; supplementation improves tendon healing outcomes in animal models
Omega-3 fatty acids (2–4 g EPA+DHA daily) — reduce subacromial bursal inflammation and support tendon extracellular matrix remodelling
Vitamin D 1,000–2,000 IU daily if deficient — supports musculoskeletal tissue repair and immune-mediated healing responses
avoid: Smoking — doubles rotator cuff tendinopathy risk by reducing tendon vascularity and impairing fibroblast repair capacity
Excessive alcohol — impairs collagen synthesis and tendon matrix remodelling
High-glycemic index foods — poor glycemic control accelerates tendon glycation and degeneration in diabetic patients

exerciseGuidelines: Begin with isometric exercises (shoulder external rotation and abduction against a fixed object, 5 sets of 45-second holds) to maintain tendon capacity without compressive loading. Progress at weeks 4–6 to isotonic eccentric-concentric exercises using a resistance band (external rotation, scaption, and side-lying abduction, 3 sets of 15 reps). At weeks 10–16, progress to sport-specific or occupation-specific loading. Avoid the painful arc of 60–120° abduction during the reactive phase; this restriction lifts as loading tolerance improves. Swimming is restricted to front crawl at reduced volume until full loading tolerance is restored.
dailyManagement: Perform prescribed tendon-loading exercises at the same time each day — consistency is more important than intensity in the remodelling phase.
Use the prescribed NSAID or analgesic 30–60 minutes before physiotherapy sessions during the pain-limited early phase.
Avoid sleeping on the affected shoulder; use a body pillow or positioning aids to maintain the abducted position.
Keep a pain diary (0–10 VAS) for 24–48 hours after exercise; pain above 5 after exercise or more than 24 hours of post-exercise soreness indicates the load is too high — reduce by one level.
Attend physiotherapy twice weekly for the first 8 weeks; progress to once weekly when home exercise programme is firmly established.
Arrange an ergonomic assessment if an overhead occupation is involved — workstation or process modification reduces ongoing mechanical load during rehabilitation.
prognosis: The majority of patients with rotator cuff tendinopathy recover with conservative management. A 12–16 week supervised progressive loading programme achieves significant pain reduction and functional improvement in 70–80% of patients. Subacromial corticosteroid injection produces superior pain relief at 6 weeks but similar outcomes to physiotherapy at 6–12 months; the combination of injection plus physiotherapy produces better outcomes than either alone. Long-term prognosis depends on tendinopathy grade: reactive and early dysrepair stages respond well; established degenerative tendinopathy responds more slowly and requires 6–12 months of supervised rehabilitation. Calcific tendinopathy treated with focused shockwave achieves calcium resolution in 80–90% at 6 months. Negative prognostic indicators include full-thickness tear (requires surgical repair for adequate recovery), diabetes mellitus, symptom duration above 12 months, and occupational re-exposure without ergonomic modification. Following the natural history without treatment, 50% of patients improve spontaneously within 12 months but with higher recurrence rates than those completing supervised rehabilitation.
recoveryTimeline: Acute reactive tendinopathy: 4–8 weeks. Established tendinopathy: 12–16 weeks of supervised physiotherapy for significant improvement; 6–12 months for full activity return in athletes. Calcific tendinopathy: calcium resolution 3–6 months post-shockwave. Post-surgical (rotator cuff repair): sling 4–6 weeks, active range of motion at 8–12 weeks, return to sport 6–12 months.
complications: Progression to partial-thickness rotator cuff tear — occurs in 20–35% of degenerative tendinopathy cases followed over 5 years without treatment; risk reduced by structured loading rehabilitation.
Full-thickness rotator cuff tear — eventual outcome of untreated heavy degenerative tendinopathy in a minority of patients; requires surgical repair for best functional outcome in patients under 65.
Subacromial adhesive bursitis — prolonged inflammation produces bursal scarring and adhesions requiring hydrodilatation or surgical bursectomy.

Secondary frozen shoulder — shoulder disuse from tendinopathy pain can precipitate adhesive capsulitis, particularly in patients with diabetes; early physiotherapy and corticosteroid injection prevent this progression.

Tendon rupture after corticosteroid injection — rare (incidence under 1%) but serious; risk minimised by avoiding direct intra-tendinous injection, limiting injections to three per shoulder per year, and mandating a 2-week rest from overhead loading post-injection.

Chronic subacromial pain syndrome — a minority of patients (10–15%) develop chronic pain and disability despite adequate treatment; central sensitization and psychological factors are implicated and require a biopsychosocial management approach.

supportResources.name: AAOS OrthoInfo — Rotator Cuff Tears and Tendinitis
supportResources.description: American Academy of Orthopaedic Surgeons patient information on rotator cuff diagnosis and evidence-based treatment.

supportResources.name: BESS Patient Information — Shoulder Pain
supportResources.description: British Elbow and Shoulder Society patient guide on rotator cuff disorders including tendinopathy and tear management.

supportResources.name: Versus Arthritis — Shoulder Pain Guide
supportResources.description: UK patient organization with evidence-based exercises and self-management advice for rotator cuff tendinopathy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Frozen Shoulder (Adhesive Capsulitis)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Frozen shoulder restricts both active and passive motion globally — especially external rotation — with a firm capsular end-feel. Rotator cuff tendinopathy restricts active more than passive abduction, shows a painful arc at 60–120° (not global restriction), and has preserved passive external rotation. MRI shows capsular thickening in frozen shoulder and tendon signal changes in tendinopathy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Superior Labral Tear (SLAP)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: SLAP lesions produce pain on deep shoulder loading — active compression test positive, O'Brien test positive — and often clicking or a catching sensation during overhead throwing. The Hawkins-Kennedy and Neer tests may be positive in both conditions. MRI arthrography distinguishes a labral tear from isolated rotator cuff tendinopathy.

confusedWithConditions.name: C5 Cervical Radiculopathy
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: C5 radiculopathy refers pain from the neck to the lateral shoulder and upper arm with deltoid or biceps weakness and a diminished biceps reflex. Shoulder range of motion is preserved unless disuse complicates the picture. Rotator cuff tendinopathy shows no neurological deficits and a painful arc. Cervical MRI and nerve conduction studies differentiate the two.

relatedConditions.name: Frozen Shoulder (Adhesive Capsulitis)
relatedConditions.relevance: Rotator cuff tendinopathy-related shoulder disuse is a recognised precipitant of secondary adhesive capsulitis; both conditions frequently co-exist and are managed within the same PM&R framework.

relatedConditions.name: De Quervain Tenosynovitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are upper-extremity tendinopathies in the degenerative-overuse spectrum managed by physical medicine specialists; combined upper-limb tendinopathy presentations are common in overhead workers.

relatedConditions.name: Myofascial Pain Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Periscapular and deltoid trigger points frequently accompany rotator cuff tendinopathy and must be addressed in rehabilitation to restore normal glenohumeral biomechanics.

relatedConditions.name: Superior Labral (SLAP) Tear
relatedConditions.relevance: SLAP tears frequently co-exist with rotator cuff tendinopathy in overhead throwing athletes; both are often present on MRI and require simultaneous management.

faqs.question: What is rotator cuff tendinopathy?
faqs.answer: Rotator cuff tendinopathy is a degenerative condition of one or more rotator cuff tendons — most often the supraspinatus — involving collagen disorganisation, failed healing, and neovascularisation. It produces shoulder pain with overhead movements, a painful arc at 60–120° of abduction, and weakness. It is the most common cause of shoulder pain in adults.

faqs.question: What is the difference between rotator cuff tendinopathy and tendinitis?
faqs.answer: Tendinitis implies acute inflammation, but histopathologic studies show that most chronic rotator cuff pain has degenerative changes (tendinosis) with little inflammatory cell infiltrate. Tendinopathy is the preferred term describing the full spectrum from reactive inflammatory changes to advanced degeneration, guiding treatment toward load management and tendon remodelling rather than anti-inflammatory therapy.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of rotator cuff tendinopathy?
faqs.answer: The main symptom is a painful arc during shoulder abduction between 60° and 120°. Night pain when lying on the affected shoulder is common. Patients report pain with overhead activities, weakness lifting the arm, and tenderness over the greater tuberosity. Symptoms develop gradually over weeks to months rather than suddenly.

faqs.question: How is rotator cuff tendinopathy diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis combines clinical tests — Hawkins-Kennedy, Neer, and empty-can tests — with shoulder ultrasound or MRI. Ultrasound identifies tendon hypoechogenicity and partial tears. MRI is preferred when a full-thickness tear is suspected. X-rays assess acromion morphology and exclude glenohumeral arthritis.

faqs.question: What is the best treatment for rotator cuff tendinopathy?
faqs.answer: Supervised progressive tendon-loading physiotherapy over 12–16 weeks is the most evidence-based treatment, achieving significant improvement in 70–80% of patients. Subacromial corticosteroid injection provides faster short-term pain relief and is most effective when combined with physiotherapy rather than used alone. ESWT is first-line for calcific tendinopathy.

faqs.question: How long does rotator cuff tendinopathy take to heal?
faqs.answer: Reactive tendinopathy typically resolves in 4–8 weeks with load modification and physiotherapy. Established chronic tendinopathy requires 12–16 weeks of supervised progressive loading for significant improvement, with full recovery for athletes taking 6–12 months. Degenerative tendinopathy with structural change takes the longest, sometimes 6–12 months.

faqs.question: Are cortisone injections good for rotator cuff tendinopathy?
faqs.answer: Subacromial corticosteroid injection produces significant pain relief at 4–6 weeks, superior to physiotherapy alone in the short term. However, the effect diminishes by 6–12 months, and injection alone without physiotherapy produces worse long-term outcomes. Injection is best used to reduce pain sufficiently to participate in physiotherapy.

faqs.question: What exercises should I do for rotator cuff tendinopathy?
faqs.answer: Start with isometric external rotation and abduction exercises against a fixed object to load the tendon without compression. Progress to isotonic eccentric-concentric exercises with a resistance band — external rotation, Y-T-W scapular exercises, and scaption. Add scapular stabilization throughout. Avoid the painful arc of 60–120° until loading tolerance improves.

faqs.question: Is rotator cuff tendinopathy the same as shoulder impingement?
faqs.answer: Shoulder impingement syndrome was an older term describing subacromial compression causing pain at 60–120° abduction. Modern guidelines now prefer the term rotator cuff tendinopathy and subacromial bursitis, because impingement is a mechanism rather than a diagnosis, and mechanical decompression (acromioplasty) has not been shown to be superior to physiotherapy.

faqs.question: Can rotator cuff tendinopathy lead to a tear?
faqs.answer: Yes. Untreated degenerative tendinopathy progresses to partial-thickness tear in approximately 20–35% of cases over 5 years and may progress to full-thickness tear. The risk is highest in people with a type III (hooked) acromion, diabetes, hypercholesterolaemia, or heavy overhead occupational exposure. Supervised loading rehabilitation reduces this progression risk.

faqs.question: What is calcific tendinopathy of the rotator cuff?
faqs.answer: Calcific tendinopathy involves calcium apatite crystal deposits forming within a degenerating rotator cuff tendon, most commonly the supraspinatus. The resorptive phase is acutely painful. Treatment with focused extracorporeal shockwave therapy achieves calcium resolution in 80–90% at 6 months. Barbotage (needling and lavage under ultrasound) is used for large deposits.

faqs.question: Is surgery needed for rotator cuff tendinopathy?
faqs.answer: Surgery is not routinely needed. A 2018 RCT showed no significant benefit of acromioplasty over sham surgery for non-calcific tendinopathy. Surgery is reserved for full-thickness rotator cuff tears, large calcific deposits failing shockwave therapy, and cases failing 3–6 months of supervised rehabilitation. Most patients recover without surgery.

faqs.question: What is shockwave therapy for rotator cuff tendinopathy?
faqs.answer: Extracorporeal shockwave therapy (ESWT) delivers high-energy pressure waves to the tendon, stimulating matrix remodelling and calcium crystal fragmentation. Three to six sessions are typically given. For calcific tendinopathy, 80–90% of calcium deposits resolve at 6 months. For non-calcific tendinopathy, ESWT provides moderate additional pain relief over sham.

faqs.question: Can I still exercise with rotator cuff tendinopathy?
faqs.answer: Yes, within guidelines. Avoid the painful arc (60–120° abduction) and heavy overhead loading during the reactive phase. Lower-body exercise, walking, and cycling continue without restriction. Swimming is modified to front crawl at reduced volume, avoiding butterfly. Progressive tendon-loading exercises are prescribed specifically for the tendinopathy.

faqs.question: Why is rotator cuff tendinopathy worse at night?
faqs.answer: Night pain in rotator cuff tendinopathy is caused by increased subacromial pressure when lying on the affected shoulder, inflammatory mediators in the subacromial bursa pooling in a dependent position, and loss of the postural muscle tone that normally elevates the humeral head slightly away from the acromion during waking hours. Sleeping supine with a pillow under the arm reduces night pain.
